content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 14, 2012


Gregory A. Cook
1127 11th Street, Suite 700
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-12-110

Dear Mr. Cook:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your inquiry is general in nature and could involve varying sets of facts (see Regulation 18329(c)(4)(F)), we provide you with informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Does your occupation as a state-registered lobbyist preclude you from fundraising for the Foundation for State Legislatures (Foundation), a foundation run by the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), which is a non-profit educational foundation?

CONCLUSION

You are permitted to fundraise for the Foundation so long as your activities do not constitute "arranging" a gift as provided in the Act and Commission regulations.


_________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-12-110
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a registered lobbyist. You have a long-standing personal association with the
National Conference of State Legislatures. NCSL is an organization whose membership
includes all 50 state legislatures. NCSL is not a lobbyist employer. NCSL sponsors and
conducts beneficial training and professional development opportunities for legislative staff on
various issue areas throughout the nation.

NCSL has an educational non-profit foundation, called the Foundation for State
Legislatures, which has 501(c)(3) tax status. You would like to encourage interested parties,
individuals and corporations to contribute to the Foundation. Furthermore, you would like to
encourage the Foundation to establish an account within the Foundation dedicated to the support
of California's legislative staff's educational and professional development opportunities.

You would have no role in the administration of or have any participation in the
disbursement decisions of the Foundation. Your participation would be limited exclusively to
encouraging contributions to the Foundation, and advocating for the Foundation to create a
specific fund within the Foundation dedicated to supporting California legislative staff. The
Foundation would maintain total control over decisions related to the disbursement of funds
raised.

ANALYSIS

Making a Gift

The term "gift" is defined in Government Code Section 82028(a) as:

"Any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the extent that
consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a rebate or
discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official
status."³

Lobbyist Gift Limitations

The Act prohibits state-registered lobbyists and lobbying firms from making gifts to
specified state officials, including legislators and specific legislative staff, aggregating more than
$10 in a calendar month, or to act as an agent or intermediary in the making of any gift, or to
arrange for the making of any gift by any other person. (Government Code Section 86203.)

³ Section 82044 defines payment, in part, as any "tendering of . . . services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible."

File No. I-12-110
Page No. 3


Lobbyist Arranging Gifts

Regulation 18624 elaborates on the prohibition against lobbyists arranging gifts:

"A lobbyist 'arranges for the making of a gift' within the meaning of Government Code Section 86203 if the lobbyist, either directly or through an agent, does any of the following:

(a) Delivers a gift to the recipient.

(b) Acts as the representative of the donor, if the donor is not present at the occasion of a gift. This does not include accompanying the recipient to an event where the donor will be present.

(c) Invites or sends an invitation to an intended recipient regarding the occasion of a gift.

(d) Solicits responses from an intended recipient concerning his or her attendance or nonattendance at the occasion of a gift.

(e) Is designated as the representative of the donor to receive responses from an intended recipient concerning his or her attendance or nonattendance at the occasion of a gift.

(f) Acts as an intermediary in connection with the reimbursement of a recipient's expenses."

From the facts you have provided it is unclear what specifically you intend to do to fundraise for the Foundation. Therefore, we cannot provide a definitive answer to your question. However, if any of your actions fall within those described in Regulation 18624, then it would be considered arranging a gift and prohibited under the Act. If your actions are not prohibited by Regulation 18624 it appears that you are permitted to fundraise for the Foundation.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Emiko Burchill
Executive Fellow

EGB:jgl
